svg.text: URDU
HISTORY & PAKISTAN
URDU ORIGINS
Urdu developed in Delhi Sultanate military camps (~1200 CE)
"Urdu" from Turkic: "ordu" = army/camp; Zaban-e-Urdu = "army language"
Hindi-Urdu: same spoken base; diverged in script and vocabulary register
Mughal Empire (1526-1857): Persian official; Urdu literary language
Mir Taqi Mir, Mirza Ghalib: greatest Urdu poets; 18th-19th c.
PARTITION AND PAKISTAN (1947)
1947: Pakistan independence; Urdu chosen as sole national language
Only 7% of Pakistanis had Urdu as mother tongue at partition
1952: Language Movement in East Pakistan (Bengali speakers protest)
Eventually led to Bangladesh independence (1971)
Today: ~170M Urdu speakers in South Asia and diaspora
SINDHI HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
SINDH AND THE INDUS CIVILIZATION
Mohenjo-daro: largest city of Indus Valley Civilization (in Sindh)
~3300-1300 BCE: Indus script used in Sindh (still undeciphered)
711 CE: Muhammad bin Qasim conquers Sindh for the Umayyad Caliphate
First permanent Muslim presence in South Asia
SINDHI UNDER BRITISH AND PAKISTAN
1843: British annexation of Sindh by Charles Napier
1947: Sindh joins Pakistan; Hindu Sindhis flee to India
Pakistan: Urdu national language imposed over Sindhi in schools
Sindhi activists resist: G.M. Syed leads Sindhi nationalist movement
URDU-SINDHI TENSION IN PAKISTAN
Sindh province: Urdu-speaking muhajir (migrants) vs. native Sindhi
Karachi: majority Urdu-speaking muhajir city in Sindhi province
MQM (Muhajir party) vs. PPP (Sindhi-based party): ongoing tension
Sindhi language: official in Sindh province; Urdu: national official
SISTER LANGUAGES, COMPETING IDENTITIES
Both: Indo-Aryan, descended from same Sanskrit root
Both: use Perso-Arabic script (though different sizes)
Urdu: symbol of Pakistan’s Muslim identity and nationhood
Sindhi: symbol of regional identity, pre-Islamic Indus heritage
Both within the same river-basin civilization
PLATE 54d ALT · URDU / SINDHI · HISTORY
